text: The Center for Minimally Invasive Surgery at the University of Kentucky presents the lateral approach to laparoscopic splenectomy. This three-dimensional reconstruction demonstrates the anatomic relationships of the spleen to the kidney and its proximity to the costal margin. These are important considerations in the proper positioning of the patient for laparoscopic splenectomy.

The patient is positioned laterally on a bean bag with all pressure points padded, including an axillary roll and pads between the extremities. The operative table is flexed, and the kidney rest is raised to increase the working space between the iliac crest and the costal margin.
The surgical team performs a wide skin preparation, covering the anterior abdomen and left flank, in preparation for the laparoscopic splenectomy.

The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum using the varus needle technique, inserting the needle inferior to the left costal margin. After gastric decompression with an orogastric tube, a 5mm Opti-V trocar is placed in the upper left abdomen at the midclavicular line, providing visualization during insertion.

Under direct vision, the surgeon places the remaining trocars in a forward array: a 5mm cephalad working port, a 10-12mm caudad working port, and a 2mm accessory port for splenic retraction in the left flank. A 5mm camera port accommodates a 30-degree laparoscope, offering enhanced visualization around the spleen.
The surgeon adjusts the position of the trocar inferiorly to accommodate larger spleens, ensuring safe access and minimizing the risk of trocar-related injury.

The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the splenic flexure of the colon using an ultrasonic dissector. The lateral attachments of the spleen are divided starting at the inferior pole, leaving a one to two millimeter cup of tissue attached to the splenic capsule for easier manipulation.

The surgeon divides the spleen and renal ligament, carefully avoiding Girota's fascia. The dissection progresses superiorly, dividing the splenophrenic ligament while avoiding injury to the diaphragm. All lateral attachments of the spleen are divided except for the most superior attachments, which will stabilize the spleen during further dissection and placement into the extraction bag.

The surgeon continues dissection around the inferior pole of the spleen, dividing any remaining attachments to the colon. The fat surrounding the hilum is thinned to improve visualization of the hilar vessels, aiding in vascular control while avoiding complete skeletonization to prevent hemorrhage.

An accessory port is placed in the left flank, and a blunt grasper retracts the spleen anteriorly. The surgeon divides the gastrosplenic ligament entirely, entering the lesser sac while carefully avoiding injury to the gastric fundus and body. All gastrosplenic attachments are completely divided.
This is to avoid later difficulties during bagging of the spleen and extraction, at which time visualization of any remaining medial attachments would be severely limited.

The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels using ultrasonic dissection, with larger vessels near the splenic hilum being secured with suture ligatures to prevent interference with the linear stapler during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a linear stapler to divide the thickened gastrosplenic ligament, which is necessary in cases of friable spleens.

The surgeon carefully dissects the tail of the pancreas from the spleen using gentle handling and ultrasonic dissection. The thinning of the vascular hilum from the lateral approach aids in exposing the tail of the pancreas. Once separated, the hilum of the spleen is divided above the pancreatic tail using a linear stapler, requiring several staple firings.

A ripstop nylon bag is inserted through a ten to twelve millimeter caudad trocar to encompass the spleen. The bag is positioned around the inferior pole and moved superiorly to fully enclose the spleen. Remaining superior pole attachments are divided, and the bag is closed by pulling the drawstring and securing it with a clip. The splenic bed is inspected for hemostasis and injury before partially extracting the bag through the caudad working port.

The surgeon morselates the spleen using ringed forceps and suction, facilitating its removal. The ten millimeter fascial site is closed with heavy absorbable suture, followed by closure of the skin incisions with subcuticular absorbable sutures.
